title: Linear momentum and its conservation
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Master linear momentum and its conservation for Cambridge 9702 AS Physics — definitions, impulse, collisions and exam technique covered.
learningObjectives: Define linear momentum and apply the principle of conservation of momentum to collisions and explosions in one dimension
Use the impulse–momentum theorem (FΔt = Δp) to solve problems involving force, time and change in momentum
Distinguish between elastic and inelastic collisions by comparing kinetic energy before and after the interaction

simpleExplanation.title: Momentum in a nutshell
simpleExplanation.summary: Momentum is simply how hard it is to stop a moving object — it depends on both mass and velocity. When objects collide or explode, the total momentum of the system stays the same as long as no external forces act. Impulse links force and time to the change in momentum, which is why airbags and crash mats work.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a shopping trolley versus a bicycle moving at the same speed. The full trolley is far harder to stop — it has more momentum. If you push the trolley for longer (greater impulse), you change its momentum more.
steps: Learn the definition: momentum p = mv, measured in kg m s⁻¹, and that it is a vector quantity with the same direction as velocity
State the principle of conservation of momentum: total momentum before = total momentum after, provided no net external force acts on the system
Apply impulse = FΔt = Δp to problems where a force acts over a time interval, remembering to keep signs consistent
Classify collisions: elastic (KE conserved), inelastic (KE lost), perfectly inelastic (objects stick together) — and be ready to calculate KE before and after

sections.content: **Linear momentum** is one of the most powerful concepts in AS Physics — and examiners love testing it in every sitting. From trolley collisions to rocket launches, the principle that **momentum is conserved** underpins a huge range of real-world situations. In this lesson you will build a solid, exam-ready understanding of **momentum**, **impulse**, and **conservation**, with the precision Cambridge mark schemes demand.

The **linear momentum** of an object is defined as the product of its mass and its velocity. Because velocity is a vector, momentum is also a **vector quantity** — direction matters. In a one-dimensional problem, always define a positive direction first, then assign signs accordingly. The SI unit of momentum is **kg m s⁻¹**, which is equivalent to N s. Examiners frequently ask you to state the unit; either form is accepted but you must be consistent within a calculation.
p = mv     where p = momentum (kg m s⁻¹), m = mass (kg), v = velocity (m s⁻¹)
2. Newton's Second Law in Terms of Momentum

Newton's second law is more precisely stated as: the **net force on an object equals the rate of change of its momentum**. This is the form Cambridge expects at AS level. When mass is constant, F = ma follows directly, but the momentum form is more general and is explicitly required in the syllabus. Rearranging gives the **impulse–momentum theorem**: a net force F acting for a time interval Δt produces a change in momentum Δp. The quantity FΔt is called the **impulse** of the force, measured in N s.
F = Δp / Δt     →     Impulse = FΔt = Δp = mΔv

items: Momentum is a **vector**: always state direction or use a sign convention — losing this mark in P2 is avoidable.
Impulse = area under a force–time graph: if the graph is a triangle or trapezium, use the appropriate area formula rather than F × t.
The principle of conservation of momentum applies only when **no net external force** acts on the system — internal forces (e.g. between colliding objects) cancel in pairs by Newton's third law.
In a **perfectly inelastic** collision the objects stick together; this is the maximum loss of kinetic energy possible while still conserving momentum.
An **elastic** collision conserves both momentum and kinetic energy — in practice, only collisions between hard spheres or subatomic particles are truly elastic.
When a question says 'show that' and gives a final value, work through the full calculation — do not work backwards from the given answer.

sections.content:
The **principle of conservation of linear momentum** states: the total linear momentum of a system remains constant provided no net external force acts on that system. This applies to collisions, explosions, and any other interaction between objects. For two objects A and B interacting in isolation:

total momentum before = total momentum after
m_A u_A + m_B u_B = m_A v_A + m_B v_B

For an **explosion** starting from rest, the total initial momentum is zero, so the two fragments must fly apart with equal and opposite momenta. This is why a gun recoils when fired.
m_A u_A + m_B u_B = m_A v_A + m_B v_B     (closed system, no net external force)

Cambridge requires you to classify collisions clearly. In an **elastic collision**, both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved — the total KE after equals the total KE before. In an **inelastic collision**, momentum is still conserved but kinetic energy is **not** — some energy is converted to heat, sound or deformation. A **perfectly inelastic collision** is the special case where the objects stick together after impact; kinetic energy loss is maximum. To determine which type has occurred, calculate the total KE before and after using KE = ½mv², then compare.

sections.question:
Trolley A of mass 0.80 kg moves to the right at 3.0 m s⁻¹ and collides with stationary trolley B of mass 1.20 kg. After the collision, trolley A moves to the right at 0.60 m s⁻¹. 
(a) Calculate the velocity of trolley B after the collision. 
(b) Show whether the collision is elastic or inelastic.
sections.solution:
**Define positive direction:** to the right is positive.

**Part (a) — Conservation of momentum:**
Total momentum before = (0.80 × 3.0) + (1.20 × 0) = 2.40 kg m s⁻¹
Total momentum after = (0.80 × 0.60) + (1.20 × v_B)
= 0.48 + 1.20 v_B

Setting before = after:
0.48 + 1.20 v_B = 2.40
1.20 v_B = 1.92
v_B = 1.6 m s⁻¹  (to the right) ✓

**Part (b) — Compare kinetic energies:**
KE before = ½ × 0.80 × 3.0² + 0 = 3.60 J
KE after = ½ × 0.80 × 0.60² + ½ × 1.20 × 1.6²
= 0.144 + 1.536 = 1.68 J

KE after (1.68 J) < KE before (3.60 J)
∴ Kinetic energy is **not** conserved — the collision is **inelastic**.

sections.content: In P2, when asked to 'state and use' conservation of momentum, you must write out the word equation or algebraic form explicitly before substituting numbers — Cambridge mark schemes award a separate mark for stating the principle, not just using it correctly. Also, never round intermediate answers; carry full precision through to the final step, then round to the appropriate significant figures.

When a varying force acts over a short time (such as during a collision), the impulse is equal to the **area under the force–time graph**. This is a common P2 graph-reading question. If the graph shows a triangular pulse, impulse = ½ × base × height. The impulse equals the change in momentum of the object, so you can find the change in velocity if the mass is known. This principle also explains safety features: a longer collision time (larger Δt) means a smaller average force F for the same Δp, which is why crumple zones and air bags reduce injury.
Impulse = area under F–t graph = Δp = mΔv

items: For a force–time graph, always check the axes carefully — force may be in kN or time in ms, requiring unit conversions before calculating area.
The gradient of a momentum–time graph gives the net force on the object at that instant.
In explosion questions, if the system starts from rest, write p_total = 0 first — this instantly tells you the momenta are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

Do not confuse **impulse** (a vector, units N s or kg m s⁻¹) with **work** (a scalar, units J); they have the same numerical units only under specific circumstances and represent completely different concepts.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: How is linear momentum tested in Cambridge 9702 Paper 1 and Paper 2?
faq.a: In Paper 1, expect multiple-choice questions where you identify the correct velocity after a collision or choose the correct impulse value from a force–time graph. In Paper 2, conservation of momentum typically appears as a multi-part structured question worth 6–9 marks, requiring you to state the principle, calculate a velocity, and then determine whether a collision is elastic or inelastic by comparing kinetic energy. Our free course includes past-paper practice questions specifically filtered for topic 3.3 so you can drill both paper styles.

faq.q: What is the difference between momentum and impulse in A-level Physics?
faq.a: Momentum (p = mv) describes the state of motion of an object at a given instant — it is a property the object possesses. Impulse (FΔt) is the cause of a change in momentum — it describes what a force does to an object over a time interval. They have the same units (kg m s⁻¹ or N s) and are related by the impulse–momentum theorem: impulse = Δp. Cambridge examiners expect you to use the correct term in each context, so practising mark-scheme answers with our free past-paper tool is highly recommended.

faq.q: Why is kinetic energy not conserved in most collisions, even though momentum always is?
faq.a: Momentum is conserved in any closed system because Newton's third law guarantees that internal forces cancel — nothing inside the system can change its total momentum. Kinetic energy, however, can be converted into other forms such as heat, sound, or the permanent deformation of materials during a collision. Only in a perfectly elastic collision (such as between ideal billiard balls or gas molecules) does the kinetic energy happen to be conserved as well. Practise identifying collision types using the worked examples in our free 9702 revision course.
